ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried spring roll as a food concept; SNOMED CT coverage for composite fried snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried spring rolls in Singapore is approximately 4 pieces, weighing around 160 g, commonly served as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Spring Roll — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient composition database at confidence 0.95, exact match to the named food 'Fried Spring Roll' (cr_id F05020048). Secondary nutrients not reported by HPB (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C, fatty acid fractions, B-vitamins, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals beyond calcium and iron, moisture) are set to -1 as data is unavailable from any approved source for this specific preparation. No forbidden sources have been used.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: fried wheat-flour pastry snacks with mixed filling.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Singaporean fried spring roll exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy to fried wheat-flour pastry snacks with mixed vegetable and protein fillings documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). Fried wheat-flour wrappers with savoury fillings are classified in the medium GI range (55–69) in these peer-reviewed tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 52.64) / 100 = 34.2, based on 160 g default serving providing 32.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 642 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; a 160 g serving delivers approximately 1027 mg sodium, representing nearly half the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried spring rolls to no more than 1–2 pieces per occasion, avoid sodium-rich dipping sauces such as sweet chilli or soy sauce, and account for this food within a low-sodium meal plan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (32.9 g/100 g) from deep-fried wheat flour wrapper combined with sugar at 14.9 g/100 g and a medium GI of 65 yields a high glycemic load of 34.2 per standard serving, posing significant postprandial glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict fried spring rolls to a maximum of 1–2 pieces, pair with low-GI foods and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glucose spike, and avoid sweet dipping sauces that add further rapidly absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 19.2 g/100 g from deep-frying combined with high sodium at 642 mg/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk; saturated and trans fat data are unavailable from approved sources but deep-frying in vegetable oil typically contributes meaningful saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of deep-fried spring rolls due to the combined burden of fat from frying and high sodium; if consumed, limit to 1 piece and choose baked or air-fried alternatives where available.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which exacerbate joint inflammation; high sodium at 642 mg/100 g further promotes pro-inflammatory pathways and tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried spring rolls and consider baked or air-fried versions with reduced sodium fillings to minimise AGE exposure and inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried spring roll (炸春卷; Popia Goreng) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian snack consisting of a thin wheat-flour wrapper filled with a savoury mixture of shredded carrot, mixed vegetables, shrimp, and chicken, then deep-fried to a crispy golden-brown exterior. The texture contrasts a shatteringly crisp outer shell with a moist, flavourful filling seasoned with soy sauce and aromatics. Per 100 g, the food provides 318 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 19.2 g fat, 32.9 g carbohydrate, 14.9 g sugar, 3.0 g dietary fibre, and 642 mg sodium. The high sodium content (642 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension. The medium glycemic index of 65 combined with a high glycemic load of 34.2 per standard 160 g serving makes it a high-concern food for type 2 diabetes management. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), raising concern for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Fried spring rolls are commonly served as a breakfast snack, party appetiser, or dim sum item across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Chinese diaspora. They are typically accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or soy-based dipping sauces, which add further sodium and sugar.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried spring roll?
How much sodium is in a spring roll?
Is spring roll high in fat?
Fried spring roll nutrition per piece
Is spring roll suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named food. Several important secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C) are not reported by HPB and were previously sourced from GrabFood (a forbidden source); these values have been removed and set to -1, reducing overall confidence to medium. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables for fried wheat-flour pastry snacks.
notes: saturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile (saturated, trans, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), cholesterol, vitamin C, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, additional minerals, and moisture data for this composite fried snack are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. Previous values for saturated_fat_g (5.33), trans_fat_g (0.33), cholesterol_mg (46.67), and vitamin_c_mg (3.33) were sourced from GrabFood, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, and have been removed. Dietary tags in the input included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based'; these have been removed as the filling contains shrimp and chicken. The input listed two default portions; the 160 g (4 pieces) has been selected as the primary default serving. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 52.64) / 100 = 34.2 based on 160 g serving providing 32.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
